title: Palindrome Partitioning
code: bool IsPalindrome(string s, int lo, int hi)
for (int i = start; i < s.Length; i++)

frames.caption: Partition s into pieces that are ALL palindromes. At each position try every prefix; keep only palindromic ones, then recurse on the rest.
frames.note: The choices at each step are the possible next cut points. From start, every end index i offers a candidate prefix s[start..i]; we descend only into the palindromic ones. Worst case (all identical chars) there are 2^(n-1) partitions, so this is O(n * 2^n) — but pruning non-palindromes keeps "aab" tiny.

frames.caption: i=0: is "a" (s[0..0]) a palindrome? YES.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: CHOOSE "a" -> path becomes ["a"]. Explore the remainder Backtrack(1).
frames.note: CHOOSE: the prefix is a palindrome, so commit it as the next piece (append to path) and EXPLORE the remainder by recursing from i+1. Passing i+1 means the next call partitions only the un-consumed tail, so each character is covered exactly once and no partition is generated twice.

frames.caption: i=1: is "a" (s[1..1]) a palindrome? YES.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: CHOOSE "a" -> path becomes ["a","a"]. Explore the remainder Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: the prefix is a palindrome, so commit it as the next piece (append to path) and EXPLORE the remainder by recursing from i+1. Passing i+1 means the next call partitions only the un-consumed tail, so each character is covered exactly once and no partition is generated twice.

frames.caption: i=2: is "b" (s[2..2]) a palindrome? YES.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: CHOOSE "b" -> path becomes ["a","a","b"]. Explore the remainder Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: the prefix is a palindrome, so commit it as the next piece (append to path) and EXPLORE the remainder by recursing from i+1. Passing i+1 means the next call partitions only the un-consumed tail, so each character is covered exactly once and no partition is generated twice.

frames.caption: start=3 reached end of s -> record partition ["a","a","b"] (partition #1).
frames.note: BASE CASE: start has reached the end of the string, so every character has been consumed by some palindromic piece. The current list of pieces is a complete valid partition — record a COPY of it (the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list, the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=2: UN-CHOOSE "b" -> path is ["a","a"] again. Continue to the next cut.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the piece back off the path so the next sibling cut (a longer prefix at this same start) begins from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's pieces. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Back at start=1: UN-CHOOSE "a" -> path is ["a"] again. Continue to the next cut.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the piece back off the path so the next sibling cut (a longer prefix at this same start) begins from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's pieces. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: i=2: is "ab" (s[1..2]) a palindrome? NO.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: "ab" is not a palindrome -> skip this cut, try a longer prefix.
frames.note: PRUNE: the prefix is not a palindrome, so this cut can never lead to a valid all-palindrome partition. Skip it and try a longer prefix. Cutting here kills the entire subtree below this choice without exploring it — pruning is where backtracking earns its keep.

frames.caption: Back at start=0: UN-CHOOSE "a" -> path is [] again. Continue to the next cut.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the piece back off the path so the next sibling cut (a longer prefix at this same start) begins from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's pieces. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: i=1: is "aa" (s[0..1]) a palindrome? YES.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: CHOOSE "aa" -> path becomes ["aa"]. Explore the remainder Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: the prefix is a palindrome, so commit it as the next piece (append to path) and EXPLORE the remainder by recursing from i+1. Passing i+1 means the next call partitions only the un-consumed tail, so each character is covered exactly once and no partition is generated twice.

frames.caption: i=2: is "b" (s[2..2]) a palindrome? YES.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: CHOOSE "b" -> path becomes ["aa","b"]. Explore the remainder Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: the prefix is a palindrome, so commit it as the next piece (append to path) and EXPLORE the remainder by recursing from i+1. Passing i+1 means the next call partitions only the un-consumed tail, so each character is covered exactly once and no partition is generated twice.

frames.caption: start=3 reached end of s -> record partition ["aa","b"] (partition #2).
frames.note: BASE CASE: start has reached the end of the string, so every character has been consumed by some palindromic piece. The current list of pieces is a complete valid partition — record a COPY of it (the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list, the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=2: UN-CHOOSE "b" -> path is ["aa"] again. Continue to the next cut.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the piece back off the path so the next sibling cut (a longer prefix at this same start) begins from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's pieces. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Back at start=0: UN-CHOOSE "aa" -> path is [] again. Continue to the next cut.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the piece back off the path so the next sibling cut (a longer prefix at this same start) begins from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's pieces. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: i=2: is "aab" (s[0..2]) a palindrome? NO.
frames.note: For each end index i, test whether the substring s[start..i] reads the same forwards and backwards. This is the pruning step: only palindromic prefixes are valid cuts. The check is an O(length) two-pointer scan from both ends inward; a non-palindrome abandons this whole branch before we ever recurse into it.

frames.caption: "aab" is not a palindrome -> skip this cut, try a longer prefix.
frames.note: PRUNE: the prefix is not a palindrome, so this cut can never lead to a valid all-palindrome partition. Skip it and try a longer prefix. Cutting here kills the entire subtree below this choice without exploring it — pruning is where backtracking earns its keep.

frames.caption: Done: 2 palindromic partitions of "aab" -> ["a","a","b"], ["aa","b"].
frames.note: Each partition is a way to slice the string so every piece reads the same both ways. Time is output-bound: there can be up to 2^(n-1) partitions, and copying each costs O(n), so O(n * 2^n) overall. Space (excluding the output) is just O(n) for the recursion stack and the path — only one partition is held at a time.
